babyFeedingAmount.name: Baby Feeding Amount Calculator
babyFeedingAmount.description: Find out how much milk your baby needs per feeding and per day — by age and weight.

meta.title: Baby Feeding Amount Calculator — How Much Formula Per Day?
meta.description: Calculate daily and per-feeding milk amounts for your baby by age and weight. Pediatric guideline (150 ml/kg) for formula and pumped breast milk. Free, instant.

babyFeedingAmount.title: Baby Feeding Amount Calculator
babyFeedingAmount.description: How much milk does your baby need per feeding and per day? Calculation by age and weight.
babyFeedingAmount.implausibleAge: Please enter an age between 0 and 24 months.
babyFeedingAmount.implausibleWeight: Please enter a realistic weight between 1 and 18 kg.
babyFeedingAmount.noteCapped: Daily amount capped at 960 ml — babies self-regulate their intake.
babyFeedingAmount.howItWorksText: Pediatric rule of thumb: ~150 ml of infant formula per kg of body weight per day in the first six months, dropping to ~120 ml/kg/day from six months on as solids are introduced. Daily intake is capped at 960 ml (32 oz). These figures apply to formula or pumped breast milk — breastfed babies self-regulate and cannot be overfed.
babyFeedingAmount.disclaimer: Important: these values are guidelines. Watch your baby's hunger and fullness cues. If you have concerns about growth or feeding, talk to your pediatrician.

faq.q: How much should my baby drink per day?
faq.a: In the first six months, about 150 ml per kg of body weight. Example: a 5 kg baby needs ~750 ml/day. From six months on, milk drops to ~120 ml/kg/day as complementary foods are added.

faq.q: How often should I feed my baby?
faq.a: Newborns need 8 feedings/day, 1–2 months 7, 3–4 months 6, 5 months 5, and from 6 months 3–4 feedings. Always feed on demand if needed.

faq.q: Does this apply to breastfed babies?
faq.a: No. Breastfed babies self-regulate intake — this calculator is for formula or pumped breast milk. For breastfeeding, watch hunger cues, wet diapers, and weight gain.
With a bottle, yes. Watch fullness cues: turning the head, slowing the suck, releasing the nipple. Never force the rest of a bottle. Spitting up after feedings can be a sign.

faq.q: What if my baby drinks less?
faq.a: Babies don't drink the same amount every day. As long as weight gain, wet diapers, and behavior are normal, that's fine. Check in with your pediatrician if you're worried.

faq.q: How do I read the table?
faq.a: Find your baby's age, multiply weight (kg) by the ml/kg/day column, then divide by feedings per day. That gives you the amount per bottle.
